As we've seen, the power of consistency is activated by reminders of any commitments people have made on a particular issue, that is, what they've previously said or done regarding the matter. Now, those commitments are amplified by three factors. When people make active, public, and voluntary commitments, they're more likely to want to remain consistent with them than when those commitments have been made passively, privately, or without much choice in the matter. So if people don't act on a choice but keep it in their hands, or if they keep an action they have taken confidential, or if they've been pressured to do something, they'll be much less likely to be consistent with it subsequently. And this will be the case not only for commitments they've made in the past, it will also be true for any commitments we ask them to make in an exchange with us. So, in our interactions with those we'd like to influence, we'd be wise to use questions or practices that prompt commitments that can be quite small sometimes, provided that they are active, public, and voluntary. Let's take, as an illustration, one such practice that operates remarkably well.
